text.value:
ADVISE FOR CONCERN: You have tried your best to comfort your child. In addition, I want to add that it is very important to look into the child's eyes, hold them gently, and speak to them all the time.

Help the women to demonstrate again using the new techniques.

ADVISE FOR CONCERN: You have tried your best to distract the child from an unwanted behavior. In addition, I want to add that it is always good to draw/shift your child’s attention to something positive. This is better for child development than scolding or hitting them. If a child does a good behavior, praise them so that they continue to do the good behavior.

Help the women to demonstrate again using the new techniques.
ADVISE FOR CONCERNAsk the caregiver to copy the child's movements, to follow the child's lead.

I want to discuss with you ways to correct the child when they are acting/doing an unwanted behavior. For example, maybe the child is trying to take a very hot cup of chai. This is bad, because the child might spill the cup and get burnt. Can you show me how you would correct the child in this situation?
Have you observed the caregiver comforting the child?
Have you observed the caregiver correcting the child?
Is not able to comfort child, and child does not look to caregiver for comfort.
Looks into child's eyes and talks softly to child, gently touches child or holds child closely.
How did the caregiver comfort the child and show love?
Distracts child from unwanted actions with appropriate toy or activity.
Scolds child or responds harshly to child.
How did the caregiver correct the child?
Does not move with child, or controls child's movements or is not responsive to child.
Moves toward and with child, and talks to or makes sounds with child, responds to child.
During the visit, how does the caregiver interact and respond to the child’s movements, activities or sounds?
For the following questions, observe interaction between caregiver and child if possible. If have not observed so far during visit, ask caregiver to demonstrate.

PRAISE: I can see that you do a very good job comforting the child and showing them love. You are very good at this. For example, it is very important to look into the child's eyes, hold them gently, and speak to them all the time.

PRAISE: I can see that you do a very good job distracting your child from unwanted behaviors. It is always good to draw/shift your child's attention to something positive. This is better for child development than scolding or hitting them. If a child does a good behavior, praise them so that they continue to do the good behavior.

I would like to talk about how you comfort the child and show them love. Let's pretend that your child is disturbed right now. Can you show me how you will act/behave toward the child in order to make them comfortable, and show them love?
What age matches/compares with the development of the child?

The child's age is months. Normal behaviors for children are:
  • 3 to 6 months
    1. Responds to sound
    2. Looks and and follows object with eyes
    3. Interested in faces
    4. Starts to reach for objects
  • 6 months up to 9 months:
    1. Smiles back when you smile at child
    2. Alerts caregiver when hungry
    3. Coos, makes noises to communicate
    4. Sits up on own
    5. Recognizes and responds to caregivers and colors
  • 9 months up to 12 months:
    1. Begins to babble and form first words
    2. Responds to caregivers verbally and physically
    3. Crawls
    4. Stands on own
    5. Walking on own or with assistance
  • 12 months up to 2 years:
    1. Walking on own
    2. Talks, by 2 years old begins to make very short sentences
    3. Begins to form questions
    4. Eats on own
    5. Sleep throughout the night
  • 2 years or older:
    1. Expresses desires, needs, questions, etc. forming full thoughts in sentence form and curious about world around them.

ASK: Are there any other family members or caregivers who could join us for a discussion about your child's development?

In particular, encourage father to join if available. Welcome them to join the discussion. Also ask the mother to have her child join or be nearby.

ADVISE CONCERN

Communicating well with your baby/child is important to support a strong connection and healthy development. By talking to our children and telling them the names of things they recognise words and this helps them learn to talk.

ADVISE/CONCERN

Playing is important for children. A child who has no opportunities to play will not develop healthily. Children learn by seeing what others do, copying and practicing new things. Play is a great way to help children learn and practice new skills!

For example, when they shake a rattle they learn what sound it makes, they learn to move the rattle.
Does not talk to child or talks harshly to child
Looks into child's eyes and talks softly to child, asks questions
Tries to force smile or is not responsive to child

Can you show me how you talk to your child? Can you show me some of the things you to do when you want to make them smile?

Indicate how caregiver talks to child:

ASK: How do you play with your child? Could you please show me the normal ways you play with your child?

Indicate whether caregiver plays with child, e.g. with toy objects or word games, or other, appropriate for age.

PRAISE

That's great, communicating like this with your child supports healthy development. By talking to our children and telling them the names of things they recognise words and this helps them learn to talk.

PRAISE the way caregiver demonstrated playing with the child for their healthy development.

Advise: Children learn by seeing what others do, copying and practicing new things. Play is a great way to help children learn and practice new skills! The more opportunities we give our children to play, the more information is given to their brain. For example, when they shake a rattle they learn what sound it makes, they learn to move the rattle. It also helps them physically. Let the child show you how they want to play, rather than forcing them to play a certain way.
Respond to your child's sounds and interests. Call the child's name, and see your child respond.
Good ways to communicate with your child at this age are to:
Ask your child simple questions. Respond to your child's attempts to talk. Show and talk about nature, pictures, and things.

Encourage your child to talk and answer your child’s questions. Teach your child stories, songs and games. Talk about pictures or books.

Children will learn words if you draw something then repeat the word.

Advise the caregiver that they can draw their own child-friendly picture books, they don’t need to buy in the shop. Example toy: book with pictures, material to draw on, ball, swing, bicycle
Tell your child the names of things and people. Show your child how to say things with hands, like 'bye bye.' Sample toy: doll.
Playing is important to support healthy development. At this age, good ways to play with your child are to:
Help your child count, name and compare things. Make simple toys for your child. Sample toys: Objects of different colors and shapes to sort, sticks.
Give your child clean, safe household things to handle, bang, and drop. Sample toys: containers with lids, metal pot and spoon.
Hide a child's favorite toy under a cloth or box. See if the child can find it. Play peek-a-boo.
Give your child things to stack up, and to put into containers and take out. Sample toys: Nesting and stacking objects, container and clothes clips.

Good ways to communicate with your child at this age are: (demonstrate)

  • Smile and laugh with your child. Talk to your child. Get a conversation going by copying your child’s sounds or gestures. PRACTICE: Ask caregiver to make large gestures and cooing sounds; copy baby’s sounds and gestures to try to elicit smile, and see baby’s responses

ADVISE for CONCERN that caregiver should try to communicate more with baby.

Advise: A baby and young child begin to understand many words before they can speak, but if we do not talk to our babies they will not learn new words. Children communicate with us from a young age, even before learning to speak they tell us what they need and they know when we are happy or sad.

ADVISE for CONCERN:

Playing is important to support healthy development. A baby who has no opportunities to play, and practice new skills will not develop healthily. We need to ensure our babies have lots of opportunities to play, practice new skills, see new things and copy what others do. For example, when they shake a rattle they learn what sound it makes, they learn to move the rattle.

Suggest one play that a caregiver can try with the child and instruct them how they can play it with a child. Then emphasize how the child enjoys playing with a caregiver.

At this age, good ways to play with your child are to: (demonstrate)
  • Provide ways for your child to see, hear, feel, move freely, and touch you. Slowly move colorful things for your child to see and reach for. Sample toys: shaker rattle, big ring on a string.
  • At this age, children have the tendency of putting everything they get into their mouths. Caregivers should not discourage them from doing this as the mouth is the most sensitive organ at this age which the children use to sense taste, softness, hardness. It is a way of learning. Just make sure that you provide clean and safe items to the child, and that they are medium or large size so the child will not choke.
Looks into baby's eyes and talks softly to baby
Tries to force smile or is not responsive to baby
Responds to baby's sounds and gestures to get baby to smile

ASK: Can you show me how you talk to your baby and how you try to get your baby to smile?

Indicate how caregiver interacts with baby
Gets the child's attention with a shaker toy or other object
Moves the baby's arms and legs or gently strokes the child

ASK: Can you show me how you play with your baby? Please demonstrate for me. What do you think your child is learning while they play?

Indicate how the mother interacts with the baby:

PRAISE caregiver for communicating well with baby.

ADVISE: Children communicate with us from a young age, even before learning to speak they tell us what they need and they know when we are happy or sad. By talking to our children and telling them the names of things they recognise words and this helps them learn to talk. Repetition is a very good strategy for a child learning.

PRAISE mother for playing well with baby.

Advise: Babies learn by playing and trying things out, and by observing and copying what others do. For example, when they shake a rattle they learn what sound it makes, they learn to move the rattle.
Who does the child usually spend time with?

SAY: I'd like to talk with you about your child's behavior and development and observe how you interact together. This is an important time in their life because the way they will grow and learn, <u>it depends very much on behaviors that you show to your child</u>.
SAY: I want to ask you some questions about how your child is learning and also discuss how you can play and communicate with your child to help them best grow and learn.
Welcome caregivers and family members and encourage them to join the discussion
Assess Development and Coach on Play and Communication

Instructions for CHV

Select 1 play and 1 communication activity from the Play and Communication Guide in the age range of the child.

  1. Show the parents the picture and explain the activity.
  2. Let caregivers try the activity. Observe the interaction and help caregivers be sensitive and responsive to child’s cues. Aim to help the caregiver and child have a successful “two-way” interaction with caregiver observing, listening and responding to child.
  3. If child is not able to do the activity choose from younger age range or suggest ways to make the play simpler. If child is at end of age range and can easily, suggest ways to help the child learn something new or choose activity from next older age range
  4. Encourage the caregiver to continue to engage in play and communication activities daily.
SAY: Now let's practice some new play and communication activities. These activities will help your child learn through seeing, hearing, touching, moving thinking or feeling.
SAY: Many people may face challenges when it comes to playing and communicating with their child to stimulate child development. I want to talk to you about some common challenges parents face.
HARSH TREATMENT CONCERN : ASK CHV to consider: During the visit, did you have any concerns that child is being treated harshly?
LEAVE CHILD CONCERN: Are you concerned that you have to leave the child with someone else for periods of time?
SLOW TO LEARN CONCERN: Do you or the caregiver have any concerns that child is not responding or seems slow?
STRESSFUL FAMILY ENVIRONMENT: Is the family environment stressful with a lot of arguments?
NO TOYS CONCERN: Are you concerned That not having toys is a problem?
On how many days in the past week was the child left in the care of another child, that is, someone less than 10 years old, for more than an hour?

Sometimes adults taking care of children have to leave the house to go shopping, wash clothes, or for other reasons and have to leave young children.

On how many days in the past week was the child left alone for more than an hour?
I want to ask you some other specific questions about your child's development.
Named, counted, or drew things for the child
Read books or looked at picture books
Sang to or with the child, including lullabies
Took the child outside the home
In the past three days, did the mother or any household member age 15 or over engage in any of the following activities with the child?
STRESS CONCERN: Are you concerned about feeling too stressed or burdened to play and communicate with your child?
TIME CONCERN: Are you concerned about feeling that you don't have enough time to play and communicate?
Did the partner/male head of household stay for the entire visit?
How many children's books or picture books or cards do you have in your home? This can be any written material with pictures
Partner showed no interaction/asked no questions; seemed indifferent about the material
Partner asked minimal questions; seemed somewhat concerned or interested in the material
Partner asked many questions; seemed very concerned and interested in the material
How would you define the partner/male head of household's interaction during the visit?
Does the child play with homemade toys, such as dolls, cars, or other toys made at home?
Does the child play with household objects such as bowls or pots, or objects found outside, such as sticks, rocks, animal shells or leaves?
Does the child play with toys from a shop or manufactured toys?

If you have concerns that child is not safe due to harsh or physical discipline, refer immediately to the Shehia Women and Children Coordinator or Sheha, who will help you contact the correct authorities or individual. You can also refer to the nearby police station or one stop centre.

Having a child who is slow to learn can be stressful. A child with delays in development may require more help with feeding and with learning new things. Extra time spent in helping a slow to learn child with feeding and with playing and communicating can a child develop new abilities.

Thank you for talking about this issues with me today. I noticed a concern that I would advise you see a doctor for. I am going to refer you to the nearest health facility so you can ask them about this concern.
Refer family for hearing and vision screen if necessary.
Compared with other children of the same age, does your child appear in any way mentally backward, dull or slow?
Can your child name at least one object (for example, an animal, a toy, a cup, a spoon)?
Does your child learn to do things like other children his/her age?
Compared with other children, does or did your child any serious delay in sitting, standing or walking?
Does your child have difficulty hearing? (Uses hearing aid, hears with difficulty, completely deaf)
Compared with other children, does your child have difficulty seeing, either in the daytime or at night?
Does your child have difficulty in walking or moving his/her arms, or does he/she have weakness, and/or stiffness in the arms or legs?
Does your child speak at all (can he/she make him or herself understood in words; can say any recognizable words)?
Does your child sometimes have fits, becomes rigid, or lose consciousness?
Is your child's speech in any way different from normal (not clear enough to be understood by people other than the immediate family)?
When you tell your child to do something, does he/she seem to understand what you are saying?

ADVISE FOR CONCERN:

Harsh child discipline will not support healthy development.Correcting and teaching the child in a positive manner is more effectivePraise the child for good behavior.Give the child choices about what to do, instead of saying "don't."

1. Needing to leave children with others is also a common challenge that mothers and caregivers face. Identify at least one person (over 10 years old) who can care for the child regularly and give the child love and attention. This individual should be advised on how to appropriately care for the child.
  2. One challenge if you want your child to get used to another caregiver, is maybe the child doesn't want to leave your side. It only wants to be with you all the time. If you leave your child may cry. This is also very common. But over time, gradually, they can become accustomed to a new person. You just need to give them some time. Don't be afraid.
  3. Encourage the mother and father to spend time with the child when possible

1. Listen to the caregivers feeling and help them identify a key person who can share their feelings and help them with their child.
  2. Encourage the caregiver and other family members to support each other in playing and communicating with the child
  3. Build their confidence by demonstrating their ability to carry out a simple activity

ADVISE FOR CONCERN:

  • Children take their cues from parents. Try to control your own stress and anger.
  • Keep children out of the argument.
  • Reassure your children that the argument is not their fault and that you love them.

1. Finding enough time is a common problem that many mothers and caregivers face. One strategy you may try, is to play with the child any time you are with them. For example, if you’re cooking dinner and the child is with you, you can try to take a short moment to play with your child a little bit.
  2. Another common challenge is that the you might not get enough help from other members of your family. I suggest, that you speak with other family members to tell them that you are feeling overwhelmed by all the work you are doing at home and taking care of the baby. You can try to encourage people in your family/household to take time to play with the baby.

1. Actually, almost anything can be a toy. You don't need to spend lots of money. You can use any household objects that are clean and safe. You can even use a little bit of fabric/kitambaa in order to make a little toy. Just make sure it is clean and safe. Now, I would like to help you think about what toys we can make just with the materials around us right now.
  2. Demonstrate how the caregiver can use local materials to make toys for the child. Show examples appropriate to the child's age. If the children are older, encourage the caregiver to develop no/low cost toys with their children.
  3. Play with child. The child will learn by playing with the caregivers and other people.
Was the partner/male head of household present during the home visit?
Who engages in these activities with the child?
How many total hours did you or any household member over 15 spend playing with the child in the last three days?
ASK: Has the child recently had any convulsions?
The child has a danger sign. Advise the mother to go to any nearby health facility within the next 24 hours so a doctor can look at the child and help.
ASK: Is the child able to drink or breastfeed?
ASK: Is the child lethargic or unconscious?
ASK: Does the child vomit everything?
SAY: No danger signs were identified in your child, but if the child develops any of the signs we discussed, you should go the health facility immediately.

SEMA: Habari mama? Kama tulivyozungumza wakati wa matembeleo yetu yaliyopita kuwa ntakuja kukutembelea kwa ajili ya kujua maendeleo na afya ya mtoto/watoto, na pia kujadiliana na wewe pamoja na familia juu ya malezi na makuzi bora ya mtoto/watoto wako. Nafahamu kuwa unajua jinsi ya kumtunza mtoto wako na kumkuza vizuri, lakini hii ni kama nyongeza tu ya elimu kutokana na ongezeko la maradhi na matatizo mbali mbali yanayoweza kudhoofisha afya na makuzi bora ya mtoto.

Greet the family and invite others to join the conversation as appropriate.
SAY: I'd like to enroll into infant and child services. I'll do a first check-up on their health today, and them return at important times as they grow up to make sure they are healthy and well. Is that OK?

Ask and listen: How is the baby being kept warm? Praise the family if the baby is kept well wrapped and close to the mother, and is bathed with warm water and dried and wrapped immediately. If not, try to solve any problems.

ASK: What positions are you using? How frequently do you breastfeed? How long will the baby feed at one time?

LISTEN: Allow mother to explain her experience breastfeeding soon after birth.

I'd like to talk to you more about breastfeeding.
ASK: have you ever breastfed?
Praise the family if the baby is getting only breast milk.

ASK: How is it going? Do you have any concerns you wish to discuss?

Discuss concerns with mother.

Is the baby well attached?

This means the chin is touching the breast, mouth wide open, lower lip turned outward, more areola visible above than below the mouth.
Discuss with the mother why she is not frequently and exclusively breastfeeding. Advise her and problem solve with her.

Help the mother to:

  • Sit or lie comfortably.
  • Hold the baby's body against hers.
  • Have the baby face the breast.
  • Hold the breast and touch the baby’s cheek with the nipple, so baby turns and opens mouth.
  • Fix the baby's mouth on the nipple. Make sure baby is in a comfortable position to feed.

OBSERVE: If possible, ask the mother to breastfeed and observe. If not possible, ask questions below rather than observing.

  • WASH your hands. OBSERVE the mother breastfeeding. Try to UNDERSTAND the situation.
  • Do you notice any problems with breastfeeding? Provide guidance based on what you observe. Try to solve any problems you identify or the mother describes.
Congratulate mother for breastfeeding in a right position.

Help the mother to improve positioning and attachment.

Attachment guidance:

  • If baby is too sleepy to feed, try to wake him by rubbing body, arms, or legs.
  • If baby is still not feeding from the breast after one hour, squeeze the first, yellow fluid from the breast into a clean cup or spoon. Feed this to baby often and in small amounts, making sure baby can swallow it.
  • If baby does not feed well within the first 24 hours after birth, see a health worker.
  • For a proper attachment of the infant, help the mother to make sure:
    • The baby’s head and body are in a straight line
    • The baby is facing the breast, the baby’s nose is opposite her nipple
    • Hold the baby’s body close to her body
    • Support the baby’s whole body, not just the neck and shoulders
  • To help the baby to attach, advise the mother to:
    • Touch the baby’s lips with her nipple
    • Wait until the baby’s mouth is opened wide
    • Move the baby quickly onto her breast, aiming the infant’s lower lip well below the nipple.
  • Look for signs of good attachment and effective suckling (slow, deep sucks, sometimes pausing). If good, praise the mother and advise her to continue doing the same. If the attachment or suckling is not good, try again. Then reassess.

If breast engorgement, express a small amount of breast milk before starting breastfeeding to soften nipple area so that it is easier for the baby to attach.
How long after birth did the mother first put the child to the breast?
Did the child drink or eat any of the following yesterday during the day or the night?

Hygiene is very important during complimentary feeding, advice this to the mother

  • Good hygiene is important to avoid diarrhea
  • Use a clean spoon or cup to give foods or liquids to your baby.
  • Wash your hands with soap and running water frequently.

Let's talk about food Hygiene

  • Good hygiene is important to avoid diarrhea
  • Use a clean spoon or cup to give foods or liquids to your baby.
  • Wash your hands with soap and running water frequently, especially before feeding your children.
SAY: If your baby is no longer being breastfed, please ensure that they are receiving a healthy, nutritious diet.
ASK: Are you still breastfeeding your baby?
SAY: Continue breastfeeding your baby on demand both day and night. This will maintain health and strength as breast milk continues to be the most important part of your baby's diet.

• Try to cook a variety of food for the baby.
  • Each week you can add one new food to your child's diet. Feed your baby complementary foods 2 times a day. Give 2 to 3 tastes at each feed. Breastmilk should be your baby's main food at this age. Continue to breastfeed on demand.
  • Drinking plain, clean water is a good habit to the baby's health.
  • Use a separate plate to feed the baby.

Your baby's umbilical cord stump dries out and eventually falls off — usually within one to two weeks after birth. In the meantime, treat the area gently:

  • Keep the stump dry.Expose the stump to air to help dry out the base. Keep the front of your baby's diaper folded down to avoid covering the stump.
  • DO NOT apply any substance to the cord. It may be dangerous to your baby to apply any substances to his or her cord.
  • Stick with sponge baths.While there's no harm in getting the stump wet, sponge baths might make it easier to keep the stump dry
  • Let the stump fall off on its own.Resist the temptation to pull off the stump yourself

You and your partner/ husband or family members are responsible for the infant’s brain and mind development, and these early months and years should be used for his/her growth.

A newborn baby can see things, feel things, and can respond to the voices and sounds she or he hears. A baby can tell us she or he is hungry or unhappy by the sounds he makes. A baby loves to see faces of her or his mum and dad (or other important caregiver). [see translation in EFFECTS job aids]

The more opportunities we give our babies to explore the world (by playing, practicing new skills, seeing new things and copying what others do), the more information is given to the brain.

For your newborn baby, you can:

[Days 0-7:]

  • Talk and Mwimbie mtoto

[Days 8 to month 6]: [use points in image in dropbox: Dropbox (D-tree)\DTree\Country Programs\Tanzania\Projects\Botnar Foundation ZNZ\Technical\Design\App Content\Protocols for Digitization\2019.03.12 Infant-Child\Shortened versions\5. Essential newborn care - shortened.docx]
Is the birthweight written on the child card?
Enter a number between 0.5 and 7 KG
Enter brithweight of the child, in KG
Approximately how large was the baby at birth?
On the way to the health facility
_Look child card and/or KMC booklet for next scheduled follow-up visit. Advise mother to return to the facility for scheduled follow up visits_
ASK: Have you received small baby services at the hospital?
This is a normal size baby and needs normal care. You will learn about care for the normal baby in later sessions.
This child is a small baby and extra care and attention is needed from both CHV and family. We will talk about some of the special care your baby needs.
Ask mother to make sure facility staff records vaccinations on Child Health Card.
SAY: Please invite your husband and family so we can explain the importance of vaccinations.

SAY:
  • Your baby was born on:
  • Your baby needs to get vaccines 6 times. At birth, and then 5 more times.
  • Using that date, here is the expected vaccination schedule for your baby.
    Date Baby's age Number of vaccinations Types of vaccinations
    Birth 2 BCG, bOPV0 (return for BCG in month 3 if no scar visible)
    6 weeks 4 bOPV1, DTP-HepB-Hib1, PCVI 1, Rota 1
    10 weeks 4 bOPV2, DTP-HepB-Hib2, PCVI 2, Rota 2
    14 weeks 4 bOPV3, DTP-HepB-Hib3, PCVI 3, [What's the 4th?]
    9 months 1 Surua - Rubella 1
    18 months 1 Surua - Rubella 2
SAY: Now I would like to review your child's health card to make sure they have received all their vaccines so far. Do you have your child's health card?
LOOK at the health card. Is the next dose to be given the week 10 dose or week 14 dose?
SAY: Next time you get vaccines, your child will get the IPV vaccine (polio vaccine shot instead of drops). Please don't be worried – it is safe and good for your child to receive these vaccines.
Enter the date that the child received the BCG vaccine
Enter the date that the child received the bOPV0 vaccine
Enter the date that the child received the bOPV1 vaccine
Enter the date that the child received the bOPV2 vaccine
Enter the date that the child received the bOPV3 vaccine
Enter the date that the child received the DTP-HepB-Hib1 vaccine
Enter the date that the child received the DTP-HepB-Hib2 vaccine
Enter the date that the child received the DTP-HepB-Hib3 vaccine
Enter the date that the child received the PCVI 1 vaccine
Enter the date that the child received the PCVI 2 vaccine
Enter the date that the child received the PCVI 3 vaccine
Did the child receive the BCG vaccine?
Did the child receive the bOPV0 vaccine?
Did the child receive the bOPV1 vaccine?
Did the child receive the bOPV2 vaccine?
Did the child receive the bOPV3 vaccine?
Did the child receive the DTP-HepB-Hib1 vaccine?
Did the child receive the DTP-HepB-Hib2 vaccine?
Did the child receive the DTP-HepB-Hib3 vaccine?
Did the child receive the PCVI 1 vaccine?
Did the child receive the PCVI 2 vaccine?
Did the child receive the PCVI 3 vaccine?
Did the child receive the Rota 1 vaccine?
Did the child receive the Rota 2 vaccine?
Did the child receive the Surua - Rubella 1 vaccine?
Did the child receive the Surua - Rubella 2 vaccine?
Enter the date that the child received the Rota 1 vaccine
Enter the date that the child received the Rota 2 vaccine
Enter the date that the child received the Surua - Rubella 1 vaccine
Enter the date that the child received the Surua - Rubella 2 vaccine
RECORD the vaccinations marked on the health card in the app (vaccinations you might have recorded during a previous visit will not be shown).
Okay, please continue taking care of your child.
Congratulate the mother and remind her of the next vaccination date according to the health card. Remind her how important it is that her child takes ALL the vaccines on time.

SAY: If your baby cries for more than 3 hours or if you are worried about how he looks or feels, go to the health facility immediately. Serious bad reactions very rare, but can happen occasionally as with any medicine.

SAY: After getting a vaccine, there is a small chance your baby may get a fever, rash, or a sore arm or leg where the vaccine was given. Give your child plenty of breastmilk. Usually this is mild and not a problem. If it doesn't go away quickly or if you have any concerns, go to the health facility and follow the advice they give you.
Provide counseling message around importance of vaccinations and refer to facility.
Give advice about the facility vaccination schedule and give an update on the stock availability on the vaccination. Work to ensure that the mother goes on the correct day.
If necessary, remind mother that all the vaccines are important to fully protect her child from diseases like diarrhea, polio, measles, Tuberculosis, pneumonia, and others.
LOOK at the health card. Are all vaccinations up to date?
That facility asked me to return on a different date
The baby is too old to receive that vaccination
Why did the child not receive the vaccine?
ASK: Can you tell me why these vaccines are important? What diseases will they prevent?

The following are very important information for you and your baby:
  • It is important to take your baby to a health facility for growth monitoring, Vitamin A supplements and deworming
Contact ID of the logged in user
Facility ID for the parent user
Name of the logged in user
You can get one for free at the health facility!

If you sleep under mosquito net you can protect yourself and your child from getting malaria.

Do you have a mosquito net?

Do you know that you and your child are susceptible to malaria? You and your baby are at most risk of malaria. Malaria will continue to be an important danger throughout your child's early years of life.
SAY: Your child is underweight. You should go to the nearest healthcare facility to see a doctor to check on your baby.
Look on child's growth booklet for growth faltering. Enter the range of the graph that the child is within.
Measure MUAC and indicate either green, yellow, or red color
Baby is okay. Praise the caregiver.

SAY: Based on your child's MUAC, he/she is severely malnourished. In order to assist you in providing proper nutrition to your child, I am conducting a referral and will return in a few days to follow up on the referral.

The child is at risk for malnutrition.

SAY: Your child is in need of special care and attention to keep from getting malnourished. During the next visit, I will continue discussing best nutritional practices with you.
Palm is not yellow or pale

SAY: I will now check your child for anemia.

Is there palm pallor? Compare CHV palm with that of a child.
In order to assist you in providing proper care to your child, I will conduct a referral for anemia.
Your baby has a danger sign. This is an URGENT and super serious emergency. You should take the baby IMMEDIATELY to the nearest facility. If it is closed, you need to go to the closest hospital.
Cord red, bleeding, or draining pus
SAY: I'm now going to screen your baby for neonatal danger signs.
Difficulty or fast breathing (Two counts of 60 breaths or more in one minute)
Excessive crying or restlessness for more than one hour, non-stop
Movement only when stimulated, or no movement even on stimulation
Not able to feed since birth, or stopped feeding well
Yellow or pale palms and soles (hands and feet), and lips. Soles should be red/pink.
A danger sign has been identified. Advise the mother to go to any nearby health facility within the next 24 hours so a doctor can look at the baby and help.
Not passing stool for a day
Not passing urine for a day
No danger signs were identified in your baby, but if the baby develops any of the signs we discussed, you should go the health facility immediately.

Explain the following regarding the importance of breastfeeding a small baby at least every 2-3 hours:

  • Breast milk is the best food for a small baby as it protects the baby against infections
  • A small baby needs to be fed more often because they can only take very small amounts of breast milk at a time

If a baby is sleeping for longer than 2 hours, wake the baby up by smooth touch on soles such as palms and foot soles to breastfeed, even during the night
ASK: Does your baby breastfeed well?

Start the discussion by explaining/defining a "small" baby:

  • A small baby is a baby who is born with less than 2000 grams and is considered to be of low birth weight
  • A very low birth weight baby weighs less than 1500 grams at birth

I'd like to talk to you more about breastfeeding.

ASK: Have you ever breastfed?
Praise the family if the baby is getting only breast milk.

Let's practice the proper attachment of the baby during breastfeeding. Could you please show me how you attach the baby?

  • Help the mother to improve attachment: Ask her to touch the baby's lips with her nipple, wait until the baby's mouth is opening wide, and move the baby quickly onto her breast, aiming his lower lip below the nipple. 

Ask the mother to show you how to position the baby during breast feeding
Discuss with the mother why she is not frequently and exclusively breastfeeding. Advise her and problem solve with her.

SAY: Small babies may have more health issues than other babies. It is important to look for danger signs, keep them warm, and feed them often to keep them healthy. We are going to learn the issues small babies may face and how we can take care of them.
Did the child drink or eat any of the following yesterday during the day or the night?
How long after birth did the mother first put the child to the breast?
ASK: How often do you breastfeed your baby, including night and day time?

Your baby's umbilical cord stump dries out and eventually falls off — usually within one to two weeks after birth. In the meantime, treat the area gently:

  • Keep the stump dry.Expose the stump to air to help dry out the base. Keep the front of your baby's diaper folded down to avoid covering the stump.
  • DO NOT apply any substance to the cord. It may be dangerous to your baby to apply any substances to his or her cord.
  • Stick with sponge baths.While there's no harm in getting the stump wet, sponge baths might make it easier to keep the stump dry
  • Let the stump fall off on its own.Resist the temptation to pull off the stump yourself

Advise the mother on maintaining good hygiene, including washing her hands often with soap and water. Explain that preventing infections is even more important for the small baby than the normal baby as the small baby is more prone to infections.

SAY: Kangaroo Mother Care is a method of care for small babies in order to keep the baby warm. The method involves infants being carried with skin-to-skin contact.

Emphasize that anyone can do KMC.

Review the following steps in placing the baby skin-to-skin, and observe the mother as she practices KMC

  1. Undress the baby except for a nappy, hat and socks.
  2. Place the baby upright between the caregiver's breasts or chest with the baby's chest.
  3. Put the legs of the baby along the mother's ribs and turn his or her head to one side.
  4. Secure the baby with a special blouse or cloth tied around the mother and the baby.
  5. The mother can then wear a shirt, sweater or shawl if she wants.
  6. Ask the mother to breastfeed the baby as often as the baby wants but at least every 2 hours.
  7. It is very important that the baby receives the skin to skin contact for 20 hours every day

ASK: Do you know why small babies need to stay extra warm in the first days of life?

  • Small babies have less fat than larger babies, so low body temperature can be a major issue. Make sure your baby is dry and warm all the time.
  • Kangaroo mother care (KMC) is an excellent way for mothers to keep these babies warm at all times. The skin-to-skin contact of KMC helps keep the baby’s body temperature normal.

You and your partner/ husband or family members are responsible for the infant’s brain and mind development, and these early months and years should be used for his/her growth.

A newborn baby can see things, feel things, and can respond to the voices and sounds she or he hears. A baby can tell us she or he is hungry or unhappy by the sounds he makes. A baby loves to see faces of her or his mum and dad (or other important caregiver). [see translation in EFFECTS job aids]

The more opportunities we give our babies to explore the world (by playing, practicing new skills, seeing new things and copying what others do), the more information is given to the brain.

For your newborn baby, you can:

[Days 0-7:]

  • Talk and Mwimbie mtoto

[Days 8 to month 6]: [use points in image in dropbox: Dropbox (D-tree)\DTree\Country Programs\Tanzania\Projects\Botnar Foundation ZNZ\Technical\Design\App Content\Protocols for Digitization\2019.03.12 Infant-Child\Shortened versions\5. Essential newborn care - shortened.docx]

I'd like to discuss disposal of children's feces with you. A child's stool can be dangerous and can cause diseases.

ASK: Where does your child defecate? Where do you dispose your child feces?
ASK: Do you have a latrine in your household?
Using latrines can help prevent the spread of disease. If possible, you should have a latrine in your home.

Explain to the mother the dangers of child stool and safe disposal:

  • The safest way to dispose of a child's feces is to help the child use a toilet or latrine or, for very young children, to put or rinse their feces into a toilet or latrine.
  • There is evidence that children's feces could be riskier than adults' feces
  • Children's feces should be treated with the same concern as adult feces, using safe disposal methods that ensure separation from human contact and household contamination

It is very important to wash your hands with soap and water before touching a baby:

Newborns can get an infection more easily than an adult or an older childInfection in a newborn can be dangerous because child immunity is still weak/not fully developedFrequent and correct hand washing is one of the most effective ways to prevent infections.

Discuss materials needed to wash hands:

  • We need clean flowing water and soap.
  • If water and soap is not available, hands can be cleaned with ash instead.

The most important moments to wash hands are:

  • after defecation,
  • after cleaning a child's bottom,
  • before feeding a child,
  • before eating food,
  • before preparing food,
  • After handling raw meat, fish, or poultry.

SEMA: Habari mama? Kama tulivyozungumza wakati wa matembeleo yetu yaliyopita kuwa ntakuja kukutembelea kwa ajili ya kujua maendeleo na afya ya mtoto/watoto, na pia kujadiliana na wewe pamoja na familia juu ya malezi na makuzi bora ya mtoto/watoto wako. Nafahamu kuwa unajua jinsi ya kumtunza mtoto wako na kumkuza vizuri, lakini hii ni kama nyongeza tu ya elimu kutokana na ongezeko la maradhi na matatizo mbali mbali yanayoweza kudhoofisha afya na makuzi bora ya mtoto.

bind.calculate: if((selected( /infant_child/first_visit_6_months/child_card_available ,"yes") and selected( /infant_child/first_visit_6_months/birthweight_on_child_card ,"yes") and /infant_child/first_visit_6_months/child_birthweight < 2) or selected( /infant_child/first_visit_6_months/child_size_birth ,"small"),1,0)

if(selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs/neonatal_not_able_feed ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs/neonatal_had_convulsions ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs/neonatal_difficulty_fast_breathing ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs/neonatal_fever ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs/neonatal_cold ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs/neonatal_yellow_pale_palms ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs/neonatal_no_movement ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs/neonatal_cord_bleeding ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs/neonatal_diarrhea ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs/neonatal_eyes_pus ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs/neonatal_skin_boils ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs/neonatal_excessive_vomiting ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs/neonatal_excessive_crying ,'yes'),1,0)

if(selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs_secondary/neonatal_not_crying ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs_secondary/neonatal_blue_color ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs_secondary/neonatal_eye_discharge ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs_secondary/neonatal_skin_rash ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs_secondary/neonatal_congenital ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs_secondary/neonatal_vomiting ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs_secondary/neonatal_bulging_fontanel ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs_secondary/neonatal_abdominal_distension ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs_secondary/neonatal_no_urine ,'yes') or selected( /infant_child/neonatal_danger_signs_secondary/neonatal_no_stool ,'yes'),1,0)

if(selected( /infant_child/child_danger_signs/child_drink ,"no") or selected( /infant_child/child_danger_signs/child_vomit ,"yes") or selected( /infant_child/child_danger_signs/child_convulsions ,"yes") or selected( /infant_child/child_danger_signs/child_lethargic ,"yes"),1,0)

bind.relevant: ( /infant_child/age_days >= 77 and /infant_child/age_days < 105 ) or ( /infant_child/age_days >= 183 and /infant_child/age_days < 274.5) or ( /infant_child/age_days >= 366 and /infant_child/age_days < 457.5) or /infant_child/age_days >= 732

( /infant_child/age_days >= 105 and /infant_child/age_days < 183) or ( /infant_child/age_days >= 274.5 and /infant_child/age_days < 366) or ( /infant_child/age_days >= 457.5 and /infant_child/age_days < 732) or /infant_child/age_days >= 1098

( /infant_child/age_days >= 105 and /infant_child/age_days < 183) or ( /infant_child/age_days >= 274.5 and /infant_child/age_days < 366) or ( /infant_child/age_days >= 457.5 and /infant_child/age_days < 732) or /infant_child/age_days >= 1098

( /infant_child/age_days >= 77 and /infant_child/age_days < 105 ) or ( /infant_child/age_days >= 183 and /infant_child/age_days < 274.5) or ( /infant_child/age_days >= 366 and /infant_child/age_days < 457.5) or /infant_child/age_days >= 732

( /infant_child/age_days >= 105 and /infant_child/age_days < 183) or ( /infant_child/age_days >= 274.5 and /infant_child/age_days < 366) or ( /infant_child/age_days >= 457.5 and /infant_child/age_days < 732) or /infant_child/age_days >= 1098

bind.calculate: if(selected( /infant_child/ccd_assessment_intervention/problem_solving/slow_to_learn_delay_sitting ,"yes") or selected( /infant_child/ccd_assessment_intervention/problem_solving/slow_to_learn_difficulty_seeing ,"yes") or selected( /infant_child/ccd_assessment_intervention/problem_solving/slow_to_learn_difficulty_hearing ,"yes") or selected( /infant_child/ccd_assessment_intervention/problem_solving/slow_to_learn_understand_speech ,"no") or selected( /infant_child/ccd_assessment_intervention/problem_solving/slow_to_learn_difficulty_walking_moving ,"yes") or selected( /infant_child/ccd_assessment_intervention/problem_solving/slow_to_learn_has_fits ,"yes") or selected( /infant_child/ccd_assessment_intervention/problem_solving/slow_to_learn_compared_with_kids ,"no") or selected( /infant_child/ccd_assessment_intervention/problem_solving/slow_to_learn_does_speak ,"no") or selected( /infant_child/ccd_assessment_intervention/problem_solving/slow_to_learn_speech_different ,"yes") or selected( /infant_child/ccd_assessment_intervention/problem_solving/slow_to_learn_can_name_object ,"no") or selected( /infant_child/ccd_assessment_intervention/problem_solving/slow_to_learn_appears_mentally_backward ,"yes"),1,0)
